titleStmt.title: Chiral p-wave superconductivity in a twisted array of proximitized quantum wires

affiliation.orgName: Department of Physics and Astronomy & Stewart Blusson Quantum Matter Institute
Department of Physics and Astronomy & Stewart Blusson Quantum Matter Institute
Department of Physics and Astronomy & Stewart Blusson Quantum Matter Institute

analytic.title: Chiral p-wave superconductivity in a twisted array of proximitized quantum wires

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: A superconductor with px + ipy order has long fascinated the physics community because vortex defects in such a system host Majorana zero modes. Here we propose a simple construction of a chiral superconductor using proximitized quantum wires and twist angle engineering as basic ingredients. We show that a weakly coupled parallel array of such wires forms a gapless p-wave superconductor. Two such arrays, stacked on top of one another with a twist angle close to 90 • , spontaneously break time reversal symmetry and form a robust, fully gapped px + ipy superconductor. We map out topological phases of the proposed system, demonstrate existence of Majorana zero modes in vortices, and discuss prospects for experimental realization.

Introduction.-A key property of Majorana fermions in condensed matter systems, which makes them much sought after, is their non-trivial exchange statistics. This forms the basis for proposals to achieve fault tolerant topological quantum computation [1,2]. Moreover, a Majorana zero mode (MZM) is a quantum state that constitutes one-half of a usual complex fermion. By the way of enabling a spatial separation of information encoded in a qubit, MZMs can serve as quantum memories that are immune to decoherence [3].

Prominent examples of systems believed to host MZMs include proximitized semiconductor quantum wires [4][5][6][7][8][9][10], and other 1D structures [11], that realize the Kitaev chain model [3] and the so called Fu-Kane superconductor [12], which forms at the interface of a 3D topological insulator and a conventional superconductor. The latter may be realized intrinsically in FeTe x Se 1-x [13], with some experiments showing evidence for MZMs in vortex cores [14][15][16][17]. Another platform that exhibits MZMs is a spinless p x +ip y superconductor [18,19] where, similar to the Fu-Kane setup, the MZMs are localized in the vortex cores. However, p x +ip y superconductors have been notoriously difficult to find. The leading candidate, Sr 2 RuO 4 [20], was recently shown to be incompatible with the required spin-triplet pairing [21].

In this Letter, we show that chiral p x + ip y superconductivity can be actualized using proximitized semiconductor nanowires as building blocks. Such quantum nanowires have been extensively studied over the past decade and are known to behave as 1D p-wave superconductors [22]. Our idea relies on the observation that a 2D superconductor with p-wave symmetry can be built by assembling an array of such quantum wires. We explicitly demonstrate this by modeling the system as an array of weakly coupled Kitaev chains. Remarkably, when two such layers are stacked on top each other with a relative twist angle of 90 • (Fig. 1) a non-zero phase difference develops between the superconducting order parameters, spontaneously breaking a residual time reversal symmetry T , as discussed in detail below. As a result, each monolayer effectively behaves as a fully gapped chiral Ginzburg-Landau theory.-To illustrate the basic mechanism, we first analyze the properties of a system composed of two p-wave superconductors, each in the form of a monolayer, using a phenomenological Ginzburg-Landau (GL) theory. A potential time reversal symmetry breaking state requires two complex order parameters, η 1 and η 2 , one for each layer. Specifically, we consider the case where the two order parameters have symmetries η 1 ∼ p x and η 2 ∼ p y . The relative orientation of the two orders implies that single Cooper pairs cannot tunnel between the two layers and the lowest order coupling ∼ (η 1 η * 2 + c.c.) is prohibited. Taking this into account, the GL free energy functional, which is a scalar under the symmetry operations of the system, is given by 4 and
div.formula: F = F 1 + F 2 + F 12 with F a = α|η a | 2 + β|η a |
div.p: Here α, β, γ and are real phenomenological parameters arXiv:2012.03986v1 [cond-mat.supr-con] 7 Dec 2020 t 0 < l a t e x i t s h a 1 _ b a s e 6 4 = " h g V J P q P n 7
div.formula: Q W P n q Y L k C Z d I 0 2 W F I 0 = " > A A A B / n i c b V D L S g N B E O y N r x h f U Y 9 e B o P o K e x K Q L 0 F v X i M Y h 6 Q L G F 2 M p s M m X 0 w 0 y s s S 8 A P 8 K q f 4 E 2 8 + i t + g b / h J N m D J h Y 0 F F X d d H d 5 s R Q a b f v L K q y s r q 1 v F D d L W 9 s 7 u 3 v l / Y O W j h L F e J N F M l I d j 2 o u R c i b K F D y T q w 4 D T z J 2 9 7 4 Z u q 3 H 7 n S I g o f M I 2 5 G 9 B h K H z B K B r p H k / 7 5 Y p d t W c g y 8 T J S Q V y N P r l 7 9 4 g Y k n A Q 2 S S a t 1 1 7 B j d j C o U T P J J q Z d o H l M 2 p k P e N T S k A d d u N r t 0 Q k 6 M M i B + p E y F S G b q 7 4 m M B l q n g W c 6 A 4 o j v e h N x X + 9 e J R q w f T C e v Q v 3 U y E c Y I 8 Z P P t f i I J R m S a B R k I x R n K 1 B D K l D A P E D a i i j I 0 i Z V M M s 5 i D s u k d V 5 1 a t W r u 1 q l f p 1 n V I Q j O I Y z c O A C 6 n A L D W g C A

+ w L L n 5 A V 7 1 E 7 y J V 3 / E L / A 3 n C R 7 0 M S C h q K q m + 4 u L 5 Z C o 2 1 / W Y W V 1 b X 1 j e J m a W t 7 Z 3 e v v H / Q 0 l G i O D R 5 J C P V 8 Z g G K U J o o k A J n V g B C z w J b W 9 8 P f X b j 6 C 0 i M J 7 T G N w A z Y M h S 8 4 Q y O 1 e j c g k f X L F b t q z 0 C X i Z O T C s n R 6 J e / e 4 O I J w G E y C X T u u v Y M b o Z U y i 4 h E m p l 2 i I G R + z I X Q N D V k A 2 s 1 m 1 0 7 o i V E G 1 I + U q R D p T P 0 9 k b F A 6 z T w T G f A c K Q X v a n 4 r x e P U i 2 4 X l i P / o W b i T B O E E I + 3 + 4 n k m J E p 3 n Q g V D A U a a G M K 6 E e Y D y E V O M o 0 m t Z J J x F n N Y J q 2 z q l O r X t 7 V K v W r P K M i O S L

C i q u u n u C m L O t H H d L 6 e w t r 6 x u V X c L u 3 s 7 u 0 f l A + P 2 j p K F K E t E v F I d Q O s K W e S t g w z n H Z j R b E I O O 0 E k 5 u Z 3 3 m k S r N I P p g 0 p r 7 A I 8 l C R r C x 0 n 1 f J I N y x a 2 6 c 6 B V 4 u W k A j m a g / J 3 f x i R R F B p C M d a 9 z w 3 N n 6 G l W G E 0 2 m p n 2 g a Y z L B I 9 q z V G J B t Z / N T 5 2 i M 6 s M U R g p W 9 K g u f p 7 I s N C 6 1 Q E t l N g M 9 b L 3 k z 8 1 4 v H q W Z E L 6 0 3 4 a W f M R k n h k q y 2 B 4 m H J k I z c J A Q 6 Y o M T y 1 B B P F 7 A O I j L H C x N j I S j Y Z b z m H V d K + q H q 1 6 t V d

Q 7 A l P z a p V b X f 9 K G i L Q x C Z 3 A = " > A A A C B X i c b V D L S g N B E O y N r x h f U Y 9 e B o M Q L 2 F X B Q U v A S 8 e I 5 i H J E u Y n c w m Q 2 Z m l 5 l Z Y V l y 9 g O 8 6 i (b) < l a t e x i t s h a 1 _ b a s e 6 4 = " q V u V c a 4 G J P Q I 9 u + k T v H F 2 + F j B Q Q = " > A A A C B X i c b V D L S g N B E O y N r x h f U Y 9 e B o M Q L 2 F X B Q U v A S 8 e I 5 i H J E u Y n c w m Q 2 Z m l 5 l Z Y V l y 9 g O 8 6 i d 4 E 6 9 + h 1 / g b z h J 9 q C J B Q 1 F V T f d X U H M m T a u + + U U V l b X 1 j e K m 6 W t 7 Z 3 d v f L + Q U t H i S K 0 S S I e q U 6 A N e V M 0 q Z h h t N O r C g W A a f t Y H w z 9 d u P V G k W y X u T x t Q X e C h Z y A g 2 V n r I e k q g a n A 6 6 Z c r b s 2 d A S 0 T L y c V y N H o l 7 9 7 g 4 g k g k p D O N a 6 6 7 m x 8 T O s D C O c T k q 9 R N M Y k z E e 0 q 6 l E g u q / W x 2 8 A S d W G W A w k j Z k g b N 1 N 8 T G R Z a p y K w n Q K b k V 7 0 p u K / X j x K N S N 6 Y b 0 J r / y M y T g x V J L 5 9 j D h y E R o G g k a M E W J 4 a k l m C h m H 0 B k h B U m x g Z X s s l 4 i z k s k 9 Z Z z T u v u X c X l f p 1 n l E R j u A Y q u D B J d T h F h r Q B A I C n u E F X p 0 n 5 8 1 5 d z 7 m r Q U n n z m E P 3 A + f w A w a J j N < / l a t e x i t > b) c) d) Out[!]=  , , ,  C=1 C=2 C=0 μ = -2.3t μ = -1.8t μ = -1.46t μ = -1.2t C=1 C=2
div.p: FIG. that encode physical information of microscopic origin and a = 1, 2 labels the layers. For simplicity, we focus on the case of spatially uniform superconducting (SC) order and only include terms that do not involve gradients. Without loss of generality, the two layers can be assumed to be identical, in which case the order parameter amplitudes can be denoted by η = η and η 2 = ηe iϕ , where ϕ is the SC phase difference. The free energy then becomes

where F 0 is independent of ϕ. For > 0, free energy minima occur when ϕ min = ±π/2, which indicates a time reversal symmetry breaking p x ±ip y order. We next discuss microscopic models that realize this phenomenology. Microscopic models.-A 2D p-wave superconductor can be engineered using an array of proximitized semiconductor quantum wires. We demonstrate this using a simple model with each wire represented as a Kitaev chain [3].
In Supplementary Material, we analyze a more realistic model which includes spin-orbit coupling and the Zeeman field effect. Both models show phenomenology outlined above.

A Kitaev chain is characterized by an inter-site tunneling amplitude t, a nearest neighbor pairing field ∆ and an on-site chemical potential µ. Consider chains that are stacked next to each other, parallel to the x axis. If they are coupled via an inter-wire tunneling amplitude t , one obtains a square lattice as shown in Fig. 2(a). It is easy to see that the resulting 2D superconductor has a gap function with p x symmetry.

Similarly, one can imagine constructing another monolayer by stacking wires parallel to y axis to produce a p y superconductor. This layer may be aligned and stacked atop the first layer as shown in Fig. 1. The coupling between the two layers can be modeled by an inter-layer tunneling g that connects the two sites in the square lattice unit cell. In real space, the lattice model of the bilayer reads
div.formula: H = r,a [-t(c † r,a c r+δ,a + h.c.) -t (c † r,a c r+δ ,a + h.c.) + (∆c † r,a c † r+δ,a + h.c.) -µc † r,a c r,a ] -g r (c † r,1 c r,2 + h.c.), (3
div.p: where δ is a direction parallel to the individual wires in layer a, while δ is the perpendicular direction. Henceforth, we will work in units where t = 1.

An infinite periodic system can be described by defining a four-component Nambu spinor Ψ k = (c k1 , c † -k1 , c k2 , c † -k2 ) T , where the c † ka creates a fermion with momentum k in layer a. In this notation, we have
div.formula: H = 1 2 k Ψ † k h k Ψ k + E 0 with the Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) Hamiltonian h k =     ξ k1 ∆ k1 g 0 ∆ * k1 -ξ k1 0 -g g 0 ξ k2 ∆ k2 0 -g ∆ * k2 -ξ k2     ,(4)
div.p: and E 0 = k (ξ k1 + ξ k2 ). Therein,
div.formula: ξ k1 = -(2t cos k x + 2t cos k y + µ), ξ k2 = -(2t cos k y + 2t cos k x + µ),(5)
∆ k1 = 2∆ sin k x , ∆ k2 = 2∆e iϕ sin k y .
div.p: While the physical time reversal symmetry T that reverses the spin of the electron is broken in quantum wires due to the applied magnetic field B, the Kitaev chain model [3] and our BdG Hamiltonian (4) with ϕ = 0 obey an antiunitary symmetry T that can be regarded as time reversal symmetry for spinless fermions (T 2 = 1). It is generated by
div.formula: τ z h * k τ z = h -k
div.p: where Pauli matrices τ α act in the Nambu space. This is in addition to the charge conjugation symmetry K generated by τ x h * k τ x = -h -k . Formation of the p x ± ip y state in the bilayer structure is marked by a spontaneous breaking of this residual time reversal T .

A diagonalization of (4) produces two pairs of bands ±E 1,2 k and the associated Bloch eigenstates |ψ 1,2 k . The ground state energy can now be minimized with respect to the phase difference ϕ between the order parameters of the two layers. We find that two degenerate minima exist at ϕ min = ±π/2 for the entire range of system parameters considered, in accordance with the GL theory with positive. In the case where the spectrum is gapped, one can characterize the topology of the bulk bands using the Chern number C. This can be conveniently calculated from the Bloch eigenstates using the gauge-invariant formulation of the Berry curvature given in Ref. [23]. The phase diagram is shown in Fig. 2(b).

We observe that the model shows five distinct phases with C ranging from -2 to +2. The origin of each of these phases can be attributed to the nature of the occupied bands in the normal state, which can be determined from the Fermi surface (FS). Starting on the electron doped side to the extreme left in the phase diagram Fig. 2(b), at a finite interlayer coupling say g = 0.4, the bands are empty and, hence, C = 0. When the value of µ is above the lowest point of the bottom band, a single electronlike FS is formed as illustrated in Fig. 2(c). In the SC state, the system can be viewed as an effectively singlelayer p x + ip y SC, which is known to exhibit C = 1. As µ is tuned up, another electron-like FS develops and each layer behaves as a p x + ip y superconductor with an aggregate C = 2. Further increase in µ gives rise to one electron-like FS plus one hole-like FS. The electron-like and the hole-like bands contribute 1 and -1 respectively in the SC spectrum, resulting in the C = 0 phase seen to occupy the central region of the phase diagram. Continuing this line of reasoning, one eventually has two and one hole-like FSs with C = -2 and C = -1, respectively, for positive µ, and finally when the FS vanishes again C = 0. As expected, when the topological invariant is non-zero, the spectrum of the system in the strip geometry exhibits protected edge modes that traverse the gap and connect the bulk bands as shown in Fig. 2(d).

Majorana zero mode in a vortex.-In addition to protected edge states, a p x ± ip y superconductor is expected to host a single Majorana zero mode in the core of an Abrikosov vortex [18]. This prediction applies to a singlelayer p x ± ip y SC and one might anticipate that in a bilayer geometry MZMs from the two layers would hybridize and form a complex fermion at a non-zero energy. For this reason, we expect vortices to host MZMs only in C = ±1 phases of our model which behave, effectively, as single-layer p x ± ip y superconductors.

To test these conjectures we solved the problem defined by the Hamiltonian (3) on a square domain with L × L sites, open boundary conditions and a single vortex placed at the origin. The vortex is encoded by attaching a position dependent phase factor to the SC pair field according to

Here ∆ ij is the order parameter living on the bond connecting lattice sites r i and r j , while θ ij denotes the angle between the line connecting the midpoint of this bond to the vortex center and the x axis. Integer n denotes the vorticity. Analytical solution to the simple model [18] indicates that for the p x + ip y case MZM is present in the vortex (n = +1) but absent in the antivortex (n = -1).
For p xip y the situation is reversed.

Fig. 3 shows our results in the C = +1 topological phase. Even without the vortex, we observe a number of states with energies inside the bulk SC gap -these are the protected edge modes mandated by the bulk-boundary correspondence. In the n = 1 vortex case, two modes appear near zero energy. Inspection of the associated eigenstates reveals that they are composed of linear superpositions of an edge mode and a mode localized at the vortex center (inset Fig. 3). Note that the model at hand only has C 2 rotation symmetry and hence the eigenstates also have this symmetry. The small energy splitting δE 0 can be attributed to the finite size effect: we find that δE 0 decays rapidly with increasing system size and becomes indistinguishable from zero in a 40 × 40 system. Therefore, we identify these states as the Majorana modes bound to the vortex and the system edge, respectively. We note that n = -1 antivortex does not show any near-zero modes, in accordance with the above discussion. Likewise, we find no zero modes in |C| = 0, 2 phases.

Experimental prospects.-During the time following the original discovery of Majorana zero modes in proximitized InSb wires [6], great strides have been made in the preparation and characterization of high-quality quantum wires and their assembly into complex structures [22]. As a result, it is now possible to fabricate T-and Y-junctions between individual wires as well as assemble wires into arrays with various geometries. These developments give hope that twisted arrays discussed in this work can be fabricated and probed in the near future.

One key ingredient is the recently demonstrated ability [24] to apply the SC coating on one side of the wire, as schematically indicated in Fig. 1. For our proposed device this feature is essential as we require single-electron tunneling between the layers to dominate. While p-wave pairs cannot tunnel in the 90 • twisted geometry, s-wave Cooper pair tunneling is allowed. Should the coating material on wires from different layers come into contact, such tunneling would lock the SC phases of the two layers together and drive the system away from the desired p x ± ip y state, which requires ϕ = ±π/2.

Conclusions.-In the absence of naturally occurring chiral p x ± ip y superconductors, our proposed construction outlines a new path towards artificially engineered structures that behave effectively as such. The Fu-Kane model [12] is often quoted as a realization of the p x ± ip y SC but it is important to note that although both harbor MZMs in vortices, they represent two fundamentally different states of quantum matter: the former respects T while the latter breaks it, the former cannot be realized as a purely 2D system while the latter can. It follows that even if FeTe x Se 1-x is eventually confirmed as a realization of the Fu-Kane paradigm, as seems likely based on the existing evidence [14][15][16][17], search for a genuine Tbroken p x ±ip y superconductor in natural and artificially engineered systems will remain a valuable pursuit.

Our proposed architecture builds upon quantum wires which themselves host MZMs. In the basic version of such wires [6], MZMs are permanently bound to their endpoints and are thus immobile. By contrast, in the 2D structure discussed here MZMs are attached to Abrikosov vortices which are intrinsically mobile objects. This mobility of vortices in two dimensions could assist the longterm goal of verifying their predicted non-Abelian exchange statistics, which is the foundation of all schemes proposed to implement topologically protected, fault tolerant quantum computation.

div.head: SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIAL Construction from nanowires
div.p: In this section we construct a more realistic model of the twisted bilayer structure that takes full account of the electron spin degree of freedom. We use the now standard description of proximitized semiconductor nanowires as a 1D electron gas with strong spin orbit coupling (SOC) and induced superconducting order [4][5][6][7][8][9][10]. When placed in an external magnetic field, such systems are known to realize the physics of Kitaev chains. We find that a coupled array of such wires forms a 2D p-wave superconductor and, in a twisted bilayer geometry, there is a range of parameters that supports the chiral p x ± ip y phase.
In the normal state, each standalone layer (labelled by a = 1, 2) that is composed of weakly coupled parallel nanowires, can be described by the Hamiltonian
div.formula: h 0 a (k) = ξ ka + B • σ + α(E a × k) • σ,(S1)
div.p: where ξ ka is the electronic dispersion as in Eq. ( 5), B = (B x , B y , 0) is the in-plane external magnetic field, σ = (σ x , σ y , σ z ) is the vector of Pauli matrices in spin space and α characterizes the strength of SOC that originates from the internal electric field E a . With the structure of SOC given above, one can account for both Rashba and Dresselhaus effects. In the most general form, SOC for electrons in the x-y plane (k z = 0) can be written as
div.formula: h SOC = α [E z (σ y k x -σ x k y ) + σ z (E x k y -E y k x )] . (S2)
div.p: For simplicity, we only retain the σ z terms in the above expression since σ x and σ y can be absorbed into the Zeeman energy h B = B x σ x + B y σ y . The σ z term is crucial because it anti-commutes with h B , as in the 1D nanowire model.

To obtain a 2D p-wave superconductor, we introduce s-wave superconductivity in each layer and work with the Nambu spinor Ψ a = (c k↑a , c k↓a , c † -k↓a , -c † -k↑a ) T , where c kσa annihilates a fermion with momentum k and spin σ in layer a. In this basis, the BdG Hamiltonian of a single layer is
div.formula: h a = h 0 a (k) ∆ a ∆ * a -σ y h 0 a (-k) * σ y ,(S3)
div.p: where ∆ a = (∆ a + i∆ a ), which should be viewed as a 2 × 2 matrix in spin space, denotes the singlet pairing SC order parameter in layer a.

In the simplest case where SOC is only along the wires, we have E 1 = (0, E y , 0) and E 2 = (E x , 0, 0) and the Hamiltonians for the two layers are where (τ x , τ y , τ z ) are Pauli matrices in Nambu space and the linear dependence of SOC has been regularized with the substitution k x(y) → sin k x(y) . This is justified because we intend to capture the physics near k = 0 in each wire.
div.formula: h 1 = [ξ k1 -αE y sin(k x )σ z ]τ z + h B + ∆ 1 τ x + ∆ 1 τ y h 2 = [ξ k2 + αE x sin(k y )σ z ]τ z + h B + ∆ 2 τ x + ∆ 2 τ y ,
div.p: The two layers may now be coupled via nearestneighbor interlayer tunnelings of the form
div.formula: h 12 = g σ [c † kσ1 c kσ2 + h.c.],(S4)
div.p: which results in a 8 × 8 BdG Hamiltonian. We find that this more realistic model supports qualitatively similar phenomenology as the spinless fermion model discussed in the main text. The parameter space for the present model is much larger, so in the following we only highlight the most interesting phase characterized by Chern number C = 1. Fig. S1 shows the spectrum for the case where SOC is dispersing only along the wires. We find that it is possible to obtain a stable p x + ip y phase with C = 1 for a range of parameters. As in the spinless fermion model, this occurs when the chemical potential µ is tuned such that the underlying normal-state dispersion defines a single electron-like Fermi surface.

We also consider a more general case (see Fig. S2) where SOC has a component that disperses in a direction perpendicular to the nanowires, as determined by Eq. (S2). Interestingly, as we turn on this effect, the system remains in the C = 1 topological phase. Increase in µ brings the system to phases with C = 2 and C = 0, as before. Thus, we conclude that the structure of the phase diagram deduced on the basis of the simple spinless fermion model in the main text is likely quite robust and remains valid for systems composed of realistic quantum wires. S1 except that the effective electric fields for SOC are E1 = (0.3, 1, 0) and E2 = (1, 0.3, 0). As we adiabatically turn on SOC perpendicular to the nanowires, we find that the gap does not close and it is in fact larger (≈ 0.039), as compared to that in Fig. S1. In other words, the system remains in the topological phase C = 1.

figure.head: FIG. 1 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 1. Geometry of the proposed device. Semiconductor quantum wires coated on one side with a thin layer of conventional superconductor, such as Al, are assembled into two arrays and stacked with twist angle close to 90 • as shown.

r G 5 V d w u 7 e z u 7 R + U D 4 9 a J k 4 1 h y a P Z a w 7 A T M g h Y I m C p T Q S T S w K J D Q D s Z 3 M 7 / 9 C N q I W D 1 g l o A f s a E S o e A M r d T A f r n i V t 0 5 6 C r x c l I h O e r 9 8 n d v E P M 0A o V c M m O 6 n p u g P 2 E a B Z c w L f V S A w n j Y z a E r q W K R W D 8 y f z Q K T 2 z y o C G s b a l k M 7 V 3 x M T F h m T R Y H t j B i O z L I 3 E / / 1 k l F m B D d L 6 z G 8 9 i d C J S m C 4 o v t Y S o p x n Q W B R 0 I D R x l Z g n j W t g H K B 8 x z T j a w E o 2 G W 85 h 1 X S u q h 6 l 9 W b x m W l d p t n V C Q n 5 J S c E 4 9 c k R q 5 J 3 X S J J w A e S Y v 5 N V 5 c t 6 c d + d j 0 V p w 8 p l j 8 g f O 5 w / l D 5 X 8 < / l a t e x i t > < l a t e x i t s h a 1 _ b a s e 6 4 = " G w g 2 + T v L N g z V P F y 2 6 f p J 1 C T I K A Y = " > A A A C A n i c b V D L S g N B E J y N r x h f U Y 9 e B o P g K e x K Q L 0 F 9 e A x g n l A s o T Z S W 8 y Z v b B T K

H 5 J Q 4 5 J z U y S 1 p k C b h 5 I E 8 k x f y a j 1 Z b 9 a 7 9 T F v L V j 5 z C H 5 A + v z B 4 w V m A A = < / l a t e x i t > µ < l a t e x i t s h a 1 _ b a s e 6 4 = " w V S d A o t G O N 5 a M C r e D g Z 0 S j P 4 P T U = " > A A A B / 3 i c b V D L S g N B E O y N r x h f U Y 9 e B o P g K e x K I H o L e v E Y 0 T w g W c L s Z D Y Z M j O 7 z M w K y 5 K D H + B V P 8 G b e P V T / A J / w 0 m y B 0 0 s a
r d K 4 z j M q w g m c w j l 4 U I c G 3 E I T W k B g B M / w A q / O k / P m v D s f i 9 a C k 8 8 c w x 8 4 n z 9 z A J b a < / l a t e x i t > t e x i t s h a 1 _ b a s e 6 4 = " / u 5 T

FIG. 2. Kitaev wire array model and its phases. a) Single layer geometry with tunneling amplitudes and SC pairing indicated. b) Phase diagram of the bilayer at the optimal phase difference ϕ = π/2 with ∆ = 0.8 and t = 0.3. The color of the markers indicates the Chern number as shown in the legend, while the size shows the magnitude of the minimum gap. Grey corresponds to points where C could not be determined reliably. c) Fermi surface evolution with increasing chemical potential µ for t = 0.3 and g = 0.4. d) The energy spectrum for an infinite strip geometry in C = 1 and C = 2 phases. The color scale indicates the normalized expectation value of the position operator along the finite direction of the strip. Note that only a section of the Brillouin zone around k = π, where the edge states cross the gap, is shown. Parameters used are ∆ = 0.2, t = 0.3, and (g, µ) = (0.8, -2.4) in the left panel and (0.5, -1.8) in the right panel.

figure.head: FIG. 3 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 3. Energy levels of the lattice model in a square sample with 20 × 20 unit cells and open boundary conditions. The C = 1 phase is shown with parameters g = 0.8, t = 0.2, µ = -2.4 and ∆ = 0.25. Results with n = ±1 vortex placed at the central plaquette are compared with n = 0 vortex-free configuration. Insets show wavefunction amplitudes associated with two near-zero modes of the n = +1 vortex solution.

figure.head: FIG
figure.figDesc: FIG. S1. Energy spectrum of the 8-band model projected onto kx momentum component. Top panel shows the two layers decoupled (g = 0), while in bottom panel g = 0.3. The model parameters are t = 0.3, ∆1 = -i∆2 = 0.4, µ = -1.9, α = 0.5 and B = 0.5. Effective electric fields for SOC are chosen to be E1 = (0, 1, 0) and E2 = (1, 0, 0), such that SOC has a dispersion only along the wires. The phase difference between ∆1 and ∆2 results in a gap (≈ 0.035) in the spectrum and produced a chiral topological superconductor with a Chern number C = 1.

figure.head: FIG
figure.figDesc: FIG. S2.The same as Fig.S1except that the effective electric fields for SOC are E1 = (0.3, 1, 0) and E2 = (1, 0.3, 0). As we adiabatically turn on SOC perpendicular to the nanowires, we find that the gap does not close and it is in fact larger (≈ 0.039), as compared to that in Fig.S1. In other words, the system remains in the topological phase C = 1.

div.p: Acknowledgments.-This work was supported by NSERC, the Max Planck-UBC-UTokyo Centre for Quantum Materials and the Canada First Research Excellence Fund, Quantum Materials and Future Technologies Program. OC is supported by an International Doctoral Fellowship from UBC.

org.orgName: Canada First Research Excellence Fund, Quantum Materials and Future Technologies Program

Non-abelian anyons and topological quantum computation
Unpaired majorana fermions in quantum wires
Helical liquids and majorana bound states in quantum wires
Majorana fermions and a topological phase transition in semiconductor-superconductor heterostructures
Signatures of majorana fermions in hybrid superconductorsemiconductor nanowire devices

The fractional ac josephson effect in a semiconductorsuperconductor nanowire as a signature of majorana particles
Anomalous modulation of a zerobias peak in a hybrid nanowire-superconductor device
Majorana bound state in a coupled quantum-dot hybrid-nanowire system
Observation of majorana fermions in ferromagnetic atomic chains on a superconductor
Superconducting proximity effect and majorana fermions at the surface of a topological insulator
Observation of topological superconductivity on the surface of an iron-based superconductor
Evidence for majorana bound states in an iron-based superconductor
Half-integer level shift of vortex bound states in an iron-based superconductor
Zero-energy vortex bound state in the superconducting topological surface state of fe(se,te)
Nearly quantized conductance plateau of vortex zero mode in an iron-based superconductor
Paired states of fermions in two dimensions with breaking of parity and time-reversal symmetries and the fractional quantum hall effect
Non-abelian statistics of half-quantum vortices inp-wave superconductors
Chiral superconductors
Constraints on the superconducting order parameter in sr2ruo4 from oxygen-17 nuclear magnetic resonance
Majorana zero modes in superconductor-semiconductor heterostructures
Robust epitaxial al coating of reclined inas nanowires
